source.title: The Waqf: A Thousand Years of Charity
source.summary: By the Ottoman peak, an estimated one-third of all productive land was waqf property: irrevocable charitable endowments funding free hospitals, universities, soup kitchens, and water infrastructure.
source.author: Islamic Economics
source.body_text: By the Ottoman peak, an estimated one-third of all productive land was waqf property: irrevocable charitable endowments funding free hospitals, universities, soup kitchens, and water infrastructure. The Suleymaniye Complex, Al-Azhar University, and countless others operated for centuries on this principle. Then European colonial powers systematically dismantled it. This is the story of institutional memory, loss, and potential recovery. Topics: Waqf Endowments, Al-Azhar University, Ottoman Empire, Colonial Dismantling, Cash Waqf Revival Series: Historical Islamic Economics (Part I) • Episode 6 of 6

x.single_post: By the Ottoman peak, one-third of all productive land was waqf property. One-third. Irrevocable charitable endowments funding free hospitals, universities, soup kitchens, and water infrastructure.
thread: By the Ottoman peak, one-third of all productive land was waqf property. One-third.
Irrevocable charitable endowments that funded free hospitals, universities, soup kitchens, and water infrastructure for centuries.
The Suleymaniye Complex, Al-Azhar University, and countless others operated on this principle. Then European colonial powers systematically dismantled it.
This is the story of institutional memory, loss, and potential recovery. Listen: https://islamiceconomics.github.io/podcast.html#ep-6

linkedin.post: By the Ottoman peak, an estimated one-third of all productive land was waqf property—irrevocable charitable endowments that funded free hospitals, universities, soup kitchens, and water infrastructure for centuries. The Suleymaniye Complex, Al-Azhar University, and countless others operated on this principle of perpetual charity. Then European colonial powers systematically dismantled these institutions. This episode explores the story of institutional memory, loss, and potential recovery in Islamic economic history. The waqf system represents one of humanity's most sophisticated approaches to sustainable philanthropy and social infrastructure. Listen to the full episode: https://islamiceconomics.github.io/podcast.html#ep-6
linkedin.comment_prompt: What role do you think endowment systems could play in addressing today's infrastructure and social service challenges?

instagram.caption: By the Ottoman peak, one-third of all productive land was waqf property. One-third. These were irrevocable charitable endowments that funded free hospitals, universities, soup kitchens, and water infrastructure for centuries. The Suleymaniye Complex, Al-Azhar University, and countless others operated on this principle of perpetual charity. Then European colonial powers systematically dismantled these institutions. This is the story of institutional memory, loss, and potential recovery in Islamic economic history. The waqf system represents one of humanity's most sophisticated approaches to sustainable philanthropy and social infrastructure. What lessons might we draw for today? Listen to the full episode: https://islamiceconomics.github.io/podcast.html#ep-6 #waqf #islamiceconomics #ottomanhistory #charity #endowments

The Suleymaniye Complex, Al-Azhar University, and countless others operated on this principle. Then European colonial powers systematically dismantled it. This is the story of institutional memory, loss, and potential recovery.

short_video.title: One-Third of Ottoman Land Was Charity
short_video.hook: By the Ottoman peak, one-third of all productive land was dedicated to charity
short_video.voiceover: By the Ottoman peak, an estimated one-third of all productive land was waqf property. These were irrevocable charitable endowments that funded free hospitals, universities, soup kitchens, and water infrastructure for centuries. The Suleymaniye Complex, Al-Azhar University, and countless others operated on this principle of perpetual charity. Then European colonial powers systematically dismantled these institutions. This is the story of institutional memory, loss, and potential recovery in Islamic economic history.
short_video.caption: One-third of Ottoman land was dedicated to perpetual charity through the waqf system. Then it was systematically dismantled. Listen to the full story: https://islamiceconomics.github.io/podcast.html#ep-6
